content.problemStatement:
After helping Santa get some exercise, you (Santa's little helper) think it's time the elves get around to actually working on toys.

Santa Claus Lane is home to NNN elves, the iii-th of whom lives XiX_iXi​ meters from the start of the lane.

You are, once again, tasked to assign elves to work on toys for good little girls and boys.

Each elf must be assigned to work on **exactly** 111 toy, and each toy should have **at least** 222 different elves working on it.

All elves working on the same toy will meet at the point which minimizes the farthest distance that any of them would need to walk.

Formally, if elves at positions Y1,Y2,…Y_1, Y_2, \ldotsY1​,Y2​,… are assigned to work on the same toy, they will meet at a point PPP that minimizes max⁡(∣Y1−P∣,∣Y2−P∣,…)\max(|Y_1 - P|, |Y_2 - P|, \ldots)max(∣Y1​−P∣,∣Y2​−P∣,…).

Note that PPP need not be an integer.

You want the elves to start working as quickly as possible. On the other hand, you also don't want to supervise too much work, so it'd be nice if there weren't many toys being worked on.

So, your task is to find an assignment of elves to toys such that:


The maximum distance that any elf walks is minimized.
Among all assignments satisfying the first condition, the number of toys is minimized.
Find these two values.


Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of two lines of input.

The first line of each test case contains a positive integer NNN — the number of elves.
The second line contains NNN space-separated integers X1,X2,…,XNX_1, X_2, \ldots, X_NX1​,X2​,…,XN​, as described in the statement.




Output Format
For each test case, output on a new line two values: a real number and an integer.

The first is the minimum possible maximum distance any elf has to walk, and the second is the minimum number of toys needed to achieve such an assignment.


The distance is considered correct if its absolute or relative error doesn't exceed 10−610^{-6}10−6.

Formally, let your output be xxx and the jury's answer be yyy. Your output will be considered correct if ∣x−y∣max⁡(1,y)≤10−6\frac{|x-y|}{\max(1, y)} \leq 10^{-6}max(1,y)∣x−y∣​≤10−6.
constraints: The sum of NNN over all test cases won't exceed 10610^6106.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test case 111: The only possible assignment at all is to assign all the elves to the same toy.
The elves will meet at point 666, with the maximum distance traveled being 444. It can be shown that this is optimal.
Test case 222: Group the first three elves and the last two elves. The maximum distance traveled by any elf will be 111. It can be shown that this is optimal.
Test case 444: Group the first two and last two elves, who will meet at points 2.52.52.5 and 10.510.510.5 respectively. The maximum distance traveled by any elf is 0.50.50.5, which is optimal; and it isn't possible to achieve this with less than two toys.
